text: Phosphors based on nitride materials are among the most important luminophores developed in recent years for light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Most nitride-based phosphors presently available on the market fall into the category of ternary metal nitrides doped with rare earth metal elements, and their improved luminescence efficiency and long-term thermal stability remain highly demanded. Herein, the local structure, luminescence and thermal stability of a novel nitride green phosphor of composition (Sr,Ca)AlSiN3:Eu prepared through a low-temperature solid-state reaction are investigated in detail. Cation substitution strategy by substituting calcium ions with larger strontium ions was adopted to demonstrate the effect of structure modification on luminescence efficiency and stability. Results indicated that all cations replace their corresponding positions within the unit cell such that the crystal expands along the bc plane, while the axial length along the a-axis shrinks together, resulting in higher coordination sites together with sufficient space around Sr2⁺ and Eu2⁺. XANES further confirms that all activators are fully reduced. The blue-shifted emission as well as an enhancement of more than 10% in intensity are obtained. HRTEM and solid-state Raman spectroscopy further confirm a specific local structural rearrangement of the anion framework, while (Si/Al)N4 tetrahedra are found to redistribute around the divalent cations in the structure based on solid-state 29Si NMR spectra. The ordered structure obtained not only helps to maintain luminescence at low temperatures but also contributes to the improvement of thermal stability of phosphors. In the case of our samples, the thermal quenching resistance at 473 K increases by 10% for the sample of x = 0.9 compared to that of the x = 0 sample. Understanding of how the neighbor sequence of selective sites such as Li, Mg, Al or Si are arranged within nitride materials is of highly importance.
